name: New Local Admin Created
title: New account {new_account} created and added to Administrators on {server}
description: Account {new_account} was created (4720) on {server} and added to the local Administrators group (4732) minutes later, outside change-management hours.
objective: Determine who created {new_account}, whether the action was authorized change activity, and whether the new admin account has been used.
checklist: Find the account-creation event (4720) and identify the creating account
Find the group-membership change (4732) adding it to Administrators
Check whether a change ticket or maintenance window explains this (simulated: check timing and creator)
Search for any logons (4624) by {new_account} since creation
Review what the creating account was doing beforehand
Disposition — persistence mechanism vs. legitimate admin work
hints: Search index=windows EventCode=4720 to find who created the account — note the Subject Account.
Naming matters: real service accounts follow a convention and are created by the identity team during business hours. This one was created at an odd hour by a workstation admin session.
An attacker creates a local admin as a fallback door. Even unused, it's persistence — check 4624 events for {new_account} to confirm usage either way.
expectedEvidence: 4720 account creation event with the creating subject
Any logon activity by the new account
explanation: Unsanctioned local admin creation is a classic persistence play (T1136.001) — attackers who gain a foothold create their own credentials so they survive password resets of the account they stole. The investigation hinges on authorization: legitimate account creation traces to identity/change management, follows naming conventions, and happens in maintenance windows. This one fails all three. Because the creating account may itself be compromised, the right call is escalation for a broader compromise assessment rather than quietly deleting the account — deleting it tips off the attacker and destroys evidence.
reportTemplate: 1. Do not delete {new_account} yet — preserve evidence. 2. Disable it and monitor for access attempts. 3. Investigate the creating account's session history on {server}. 4. Escalate to IR for compromise assessment of {server}.

events.message: An account was successfully logged on. Account Name: {user} Logon Type: 10 Source Network Address: {src_ip2}
A user account was created. New Account Name: {new_account} Subject Account Name: {user}
A member was added to a security-enabled local group. Group Name: Administrators Member: {new_account} Subject Account Name: {user}
